MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-22-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom Conference
Jason Michael Rose, Esq., Brayton Purcell LLP 415-898-1555 jrose@braytonlaw.com Attorney for Plaintiff, ESTEBAN SAMORA SERRANO
RYAN H. CHAN, Esq., BARNES & THORNBURG LLP (310) 284-3880 Ryan.Chan@btlaw.com Attorneys for Defendant, COSTCO WHOLESALE CORPORATION

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT COSTCO WHOLESALE CORPORATION's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Granted in part.

1. Reference to Superior Court orders

Plaintiff cites superior court orders. Oppos. at 2:10. These are irrelevant: they have no precedential effect. Schmidt v. Bank of Am., N.A., 223 Cal. App. 4th 1489, 1509 & n.16 (2014); Pep Boys Manny Moe & Jack of California v. Old Republic Ins. Co., 98 Cal. App. 5th 329, 341 n.5 (2023).

2. Merits

As with the demurrers, Costco's motion to strike focuses exclusively on allegations which expressly call out Costco by name, and does not address allegations that apply to defendants indiscriminately. E.g., Memorandum In Support at 10:2 ("specific to Costco"). The allegations specific to Costco's officers (used to allege punitive damages against the company) are based on information and belief, Complaint 571. Arguably, such allegations must be supported by other allegations in the complaint of the information on which the belief is based. E.g., Gomes v. Countrywide Home Loans, Inc., 192 Cal. App. 4th 1149, 1158-59 (2011); Brown v. USA Taekwondo, 40 Cal. App. 5th 1077, 1106 (same). But Costco has not argued this. What is clear, however, as noted in connection with Costco's demurrers, is that as a matter of fact (i) there does need to be a good faith belief, and (ii) that belief must be supported by something other than speculation. Again, early discovery should disclose the extent to which plaintiff can support his allegations and thus the extent to which the complaint against Cosco was brought in good faith and is not frivolous.

Plaintiff notes a series of sections in the complaint which apply to all defendants, and if the facts alleged turn out to be true, they may support the claim for punitive damages. (Some of plaintiff's argument doesn't apply to Costco, and seems lifted from papers that perhaps were filed in some other case. See Opposition at 10:7-26 (re manufactures).)

Separately, Costco moves to strike portions of the complaint at 33:18. Memorandum at 19. The allegations violate the requirements of Bockrath v. Aldrich Chem. Co., 21 Cal. 4th 71 (1999). Plaintiff never mentions this in the Opposition, and the issue is conceded. The motion to strike is granted to that extent.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/CEK)
